paper_title: Benthic Diatom Community Assembly in Xiangxi River

methods.description: Statistical and ecological analyses performed on collected benthic diatom community data using R, including decomposition of beta-diversity, Mantel tests, and calculation of DNCI values to assess community assembly processes.

steps.description: R (version 4.0.2) (R Core Team, 2020) was used to perform all analyses.
steps.searchable_summary: Set up R environment with specified version

steps.description: Using the beta.pair function in the R package betapart, based on presenceabsence data (using Sørensen distance), the total taxonomybased β-diversity was divided into two parts: turnover and nestedness (Baselga and Orme, 2012).
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate taxonomy-based beta diversity components (total, turnover, nestedness) using Sørensen distance on presence-absence data
outputs: Taxonomy-based beta diversity matrix (total, turnover, nestedness)

steps.description: First, the gowdis function in the R package FD was used to calculate the distance between species based on trait data.
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate Gower distance matrix for species based on trait data

steps.description: Second, the Gower distance was used for principal coordinate analysis (PCoA), and the pco function in the R package labdsv was used to generate the trait vector.
steps.searchable_summary: Perform Principal Coordinate Analysis (PCoA) on species Gower distance matrix to obtain trait vectors

steps.description: Third, the function functional.beta.pair in the R package betapart was used to generate three trait-based β-diversity matrices, using the first two PCoA vectors and species data.
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate trait-based beta diversity components (total, turnover, nestedness) using functional beta pair analysis with PCoA vectors
outputs: Trait-based beta diversity matrices (total, turnover, nestedness)

steps.description: By decomposing β-diversity, we can know the relative contribution of turnover and nestedness components to the total taxonomyand trait-based β-diversity, and compare the differences among the 5 different habitats (H01-H05) (question i).
steps.searchable_summary: Compare beta diversity components across different habitat groups
outputs: Comparison results of beta diversity components across habitats

steps.description: Furthermore, we ran Mantel test (using the function mantel in R package vegan) (Oksanen et al., 2019) to test the relationship between taxonomy-and trait-based β-diversity components to examine whether both facets of β-diversity provide complementarity of ecological information.
steps.searchable_summary: Perform Mantel test to correlate taxonomy-based and trait-based beta diversity matrices

steps.description: To explore what is the key mechanism of community assembly processes in the study area impacted by small run-of-river dams (question ii) and how do they affect community assembly processes of benthic diatoms (question iii), we calculated and compared the pairwise DNCI values (function DNCI_multigroup in R package DNCImper) (Gibert, 2021) for both species and trait composition of benthic diatoms among 5 different habitats.
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate and compare pairwise DNCI values for species and trait composition across habitat groups
outputs: Comparison results of DNCI values across habitats

steps.description: In order to produce more robust results, we made pairs of groups even by random sampling of the largest groups (i.e., symmetrize = TRUE).
steps.searchable_summary: Apply symmetrization by random sampling for DNCI calculation

methods.method_name: Benthic Algae Field Sampling and Processing
methods.description: Procedure for collecting benthic algae samples from river stones, preserving them, and processing them for microscopic identification of nondiatoms and diatoms.

steps.description: Algae samples were collected at the 90 sites.
steps.searchable_summary: Conduct field sampling at specified sites

steps.description: At each sample point, three to five representative stones with a diameter of ∼25 cm were selected...
steps.searchable_summary: Select representative stones for sampling at each site

steps.description: ...and the algae in the sampling area with a diameter of 2.7 cm was brushed...
steps.searchable_summary: Collect algae from a defined area on selected stones using brushing

steps.description: ...and then rinsed with 350 ml distilled water.
steps.searchable_summary: Rinse collected algae material with distilled water

steps.description: Algae samples were then preserved with 4% formalin for subsequent identification.
steps.searchable_summary: Preserve algae samples using 4% formalin solution

steps.description: First, a 400× magnification microscope was used to identify nondiatoms in the 0.1 ml counting chamber.
steps.searchable_summary: Identify and count nondiatom algae under microscope using a counting chamber

steps.description: Second, permanent diatom slides were prepared with nitric acid and sulfuric acid.
steps.searchable_summary: Prepare permanent diatom slides using acid digestion

steps.description: We counted at least 300 diatom valves using a 1,000× magnification microscope under oil immersion conditions.
steps.searchable_summary: Count diatom valves under high magnification microscope
outputs: Diatom count data (at least 300 valves per sample)

steps.description: We used available information to determine algae as the lowest classification level from Anonymous (1992). Hu et al. (1980), Zhang and Huang (1991), and Zhu (2000).
steps.searchable_summary: Classify identified algae to the lowest possible taxonomic level using reference literature
inputs: Reference literature (Anonymous 1992, Hu et al. 1980, Zhang and Huang 1991, Zhu 2000)

steps.description: Ind/m 2 represented the unit of density of benthic algae.
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate benthic algae density in individuals per square meter
